explicit_text: My name is Toyo Kazato, born on August 20, 1887, in Iwakuni, Yamaguchi Prefecture during the Meiji period. I came to America in 1906 when I was 20 years old, shortly after a big earthquake had damaged San Francisco. I grew up in a poor samurai family, and despite many hardships, I pursued education and eventually traveled to the United States to improve my English and opportunities.

At age 20, in 1906, I traveled to America shortly after a major earthquake devastated a West Coast city. Coming from a poor samurai family, I faced many hardships but pursued education and sought better opportunities by improving my English abroad.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Iwakuni, Yamaguchi Prefecture, where my family lived after my father, a poor samurai, passed away when I was four. Iwakuni was a place where there was a Methodist church and a Presbyterian outpost that influenced my early religious and educational experiences. It was also a community where people valued military service highly, as seen in my older brother's path.

It was also a community that highly valued military service, as reflected in my older brother's career path.

explicit_text: Yamaguchi Prefecture was where I attended a mission school about 20 ri from my home in Iwakuni. My older brother also studied there, attending middle and high school in Yamaguchi before becoming a military officer. The prefecture was a center for education and military aspirations during the time I grew up.

explicit_text: I was born in the 19th year of the Meiji period, 1887, a time when Japan was transitioning from the feudal system. My father was a samurai, but the feudal system had ended, and he received some money which he used to buy rice fields. This period shaped my family's social and economic conditions and influenced my upbringing.

explicit_text: I arrived in San Francisco in the autumn of 1906, shortly after the big earthquake in the spring that had devastated the city. San Francisco was still torn up when I got there, and it was my first impression of America. From there, I traveled on to Berkeley to continue my education and new life.

explicit_text: In Iwakuni, there was a Methodist church that played a role in my early religious life. It had an outpost for evangelism where a man came to hold meetings, and I was taken there for Sunday school by my neighbor. This church was part of the Christian mission presence that eventually led me to attend a mission school in Yamaguchi.

explicit_text: Joshi Gakuin was a women's college in Tokyo that two graduates from my mission school received scholarships to attend. I had hoped to go there with a friend, but my older brother was seriously wounded in the Russo-Japanese War and I stayed behind to care for him. Although I missed the chance to attend Joshi Gakuin, it remained a goal I had before coming to America.

explicit_text: The Russo-Japanese War started just as I was preparing to attend Joshi Gakuin. My older brother went to fight in the war and came back seriously wounded, requiring treatment at Ujina. Because of his condition, I stayed home to care for him, which prevented me from going to college at that time.

explicit_text: Ujina was the place where my older brother was sent for treatment after being seriously wounded in the Russo-Japanese War. It was far from the battlefield and allowed him to recover somewhat at home. His time there influenced my decision to stay in Japan and care for him instead of going to college.

explicit_text: I came to the United States in 1906 with a missionary who was returning home. I wanted to improve my English and believed coming to America was a better opportunity than going to college in Japan at that time. My arrival coincided with the aftermath of the San Francisco earthquake, and I eventually made my way to Berkeley and then Fresno.

My arrival coincided with the aftermath of a major earthquake on the West Coast, and I eventually made my way to a university town and then to a city in California's Central Valley.

explicit_text: When I arrived in America, I was sent to Berkeley to stay with a family connected to a professor of Zoology who had been in Japan. I was told to go to East Hall on the university campus, but arriving late and alone was a challenging experience. A kind young man named Dale helped me find Dr. Litter's house, where I was welcomed and cared for.

explicit_text: On my way to Dr. Litter's house in Berkeley, the young man who helped me pointed out the YWCA, explaining it was a place where girls gathered and had a good time. This was my first introduction to American social organizations for women, and it helped me feel more connected to the community. It was part of the new world I was entering in the United States.
